title: To Helen by Edgar Allan Poe — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
I saw thee once--once only--years ago:
I must not say _how_ many--but _not_ many.
It was a July midnight; and from out
A full-orbed moon, that, like thine own soul, soaring,
Sought a precipitate pathway up through heaven,
There fell a silvery-silken veil of light,
With quietude, and sultriness and slumber,
Upon the upturn'd faces of a thousand
meaning: "To Helen" by Edgar Allan Poe is a sad and love and deep and nature poem consisting of 70 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "I saw thee once--once only--years ago: I must not say _how_ many--but _not_ many....", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Edgar Allan Poe's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
